news_summary: Advancements in cellular research, innovative CAR T cell treatments, pioneering stem-cell therapy, and the shingles vaccine collectively offer promising breakthroughs in combating cancer, neurological disorders, and aging-related inflammation.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans la recherche cellulaire, les traitements innovants à base de cellules CAR-T, les thérapies pionnières à base de cellules souches et le vaccin contre le zona offrent collectivement des avancées prometteuses dans la lutte contre le cancer, les troubles neurologiques et l'inflammation liée au vieillissement.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la investigación celular, los innovadores tratamientos con células CAR-T, la terapia pionera con células madre y la vacuna contra el herpes zóster ofrecen, en conjunto, avances prometedores en la lucha contra el cáncer, los trastornos neurológicos y la inflamación relacionada con el envejecimiento.

articles.title: Hidden architecture inside cellular droplets opens new targets for cancer and ALS
articles.summary: Biomolecular condensates were long believed to be simple liquid blobs inside cells. Researchers have now uncovered that some are actually supported by fine protein filaments forming an internal scaffold. When this structure is disrupted, cells fail to grow and divide properly. The discovery suggests scientists may one day design drugs that target condensate architecture to fight cancer and neurodegenerative disease.
articles.content:
Cells organize many of their most important activities using structures known as biomolecular condensates. Unlike traditional compartments in the cell, these droplet-like clusters are not enclosed by membranes. They help control how genetic instructions in DNA are converted into proteins, assist in clearing away cellular waste that could otherwise become toxic, and can even play a role in suppressing tumor growth. Because condensates behave like liquids that can fuse, flow, and quickly exchange components, scientists long believed they were simple, unstructured droplets.

Research published in Nature Structural and Molecular Biology on February 2, 2026, challenges that long-standing view. A team at Scripps Research found that some condensates are not random blobs at all. Instead, they are built from complex networks of thin, thread-like protein filaments. These internal frameworks give the droplets a defined architecture that is crucial for how they work. The discovery points to new strategies for treating diseases such as cancer and neurodegenerative disorders.

"Ever since we realized that disruptions in condensate formation are at the heart of many diseases, it has been challenging to target them therapeutically because they appeared to lack structure -- there were no specific features for a drug to latch onto," says Keren Lasker, associate professor at Scripps Research and senior author of the study. "This work changes that. We can now see that some condensates have an internal architecture, and that, importantly, this structure is required for function, opening the door to targeting these membrane-less assemblies much like we target individual proteins."

Zooming In on the PopZ Protein

To explore how condensates can act like compartments without membranes, Lasker's lab examined a bacterial protein called PopZ. In certain rod-shaped bacteria, PopZ gathers at the cell poles (the rounded ends of the cell), forming condensates that organize other proteins needed for cell division.

Working closely with Scripps Research professor Ashok Deniz and assistant professor Raphael Park, who co-led the study, the team used cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET). This imaging method functions much like a CT scan at the molecular scale, allowing researchers to see cellular structures in remarkable detail. The images revealed that PopZ proteins assemble into filaments through a carefully ordered, step-by-step process. These filaments then form a scaffold that determines the condensate's physical characteristics.

Protein Shape Changes Inside Condensates

The researchers went further to examine how individual PopZ molecules behave. Using single-molecule Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), a technique that detects tiny shifts in distance within proteins by measuring energy transfer between fluorescent tags, they discovered that PopZ changes shape depending on its location. The protein adopts one conformation outside a condensate and a different one inside it.

"Realizing that protein conformation depends on location gives us multiple ways to engineer cellular function," says Daniel Scholl, first author and former postdoctoral researcher in the Lasker and Deniz labs.

Why Filament Structure Is Essential

To test whether the filaments were merely structural details or actually necessary for life, the team engineered a mutant version of PopZ that could no longer form filaments. The altered condensates became much more fluid and had lower surface tension. When these changes were introduced into living bacteria, the cells stopped growing and failed to properly separate their DNA. This showed that the condensate's physical properties, not just its chemical ingredients, are vital for normal cellular function.

Implications for Cancer and Neurodegenerative Disease

Although the experiments focused on bacteria, the findings have broader relevance. In human cells, filament-based condensates carry out two major tasks: clearing away damaged or toxic proteins and controlling cell growth. If the cleanup condensates break down, harmful proteins can build up, which is a defining feature of neurodegenerative diseases such as ALS. If growth-regulating condensates fail, the protective mechanisms that prevent tumors can collapse, contributing to cancers including prostate, breast and endometrial.

"By demonstrating that condensate architecture is both definable and functionally critical, the work raises the possibility of designing therapies that act directly on condensate structure and correct the underlying disorganization that allows disease to take hold," says Lasker.

In addition to Lasker, Scholl, Deniz, and Park, authors of the study, "The filamentous ultrastructure of the PopZ condensate is required for its cellular function," include Tumara Boyd, Andrew P. Latham, Alexandra Salazar, Asma Khan, Steven Boeynaems, Alex S. Holehouse, Gabriel C. Lander and Andrej Sali.

The research was supported by the National Institutes of Health (NINDS DP2 NS142714, NIGMS F32 GM150243, NIGMS R01 GM083960, NINDS R01 NS095892, NIGMS RO1 GM14305, NIGMS R35 GM130375, and ORIPS10 OD032467), the National Science Foundation (2235200 and DBI 2213983), the Water and Life Interface Institute, the Gordon & Betty Moore Foundation (Moore Inventor Fellowship 579361), and the Cancer Prevention and Research Institute of Texas (RR220094).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing internal architecture in cellular biomolecular condensates, overturning previous assumptions. This breakthrough has broad implications for developing new therapies targeting cancer and neurodegenerative diseases like ALS, offering hope for improved treatments. The research is detailed, focused, and highlights a novel mechanism with potential large-scale health benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover hidden structure inside cell droplets linked to cancer and ALS
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Inside our cells, tiny droplet-like groups called biomolecular condensates help manage important jobs like making proteins and cleaning up waste. Scientists used to think these droplets were simple blobs without any special shape.

**What happened** – Researchers at Scripps Research found that some of these droplets actually have an inner scaffold made of thin protein threads. They studied a bacterial protein called PopZ using a special imaging method similar to a tiny CT scan. They saw that PopZ proteins form filaments that create a framework inside the droplets. When the filaments were removed, cells could not grow or divide properly.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that condensates have a clear structure needed for their function. It changes how scientists think about these droplets and opens new ways to design drugs. Instead of treating condensates as shapeless blobs, medicines could target their internal scaffolds to help fight diseases like cancer and ALS.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore how this filament structure works in human cells and how drugs can be developed to fix problems in condensates. This could lead to new treatments that stop harmful proteins from building up or help control cell growth more effectively.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Finding a hidden protein scaffold inside cell droplets reveals new drug targets to treat cancer and neurodegenerative diseases like ALS.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des chercheurs découvrent une structure cachée dans les gouttelettes cellulaires liée au cancer et à la SLA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – À l'intérieur de nos cellules, de petites gouttelettes appelées condensats biomoléculaires aident à gérer des tâches importantes comme la fabrication des protéines et le nettoyage des déchets. Les scientifiques pensaient auparavant que ces gouttelettes étaient de simples amas sans forme particulière.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Scripps Research ont découvert que certaines de ces gouttelettes ont en réalité un échafaudage interne fait de fins fils de protéines. Ils ont étudié une protéine bactérienne appelée PopZ avec une méthode d'imagerie spéciale, semblable à un scanner miniature. Ils ont vu que les protéines PopZ forment des filaments qui créent une structure à l'intérieur des gouttelettes. Lorsque ces filaments ont été supprimés, les cellules ne pouvaient plus croître ni se diviser correctement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les condensats ont une structure claire nécessaire à leur fonctionnement. Elle change la façon dont les scientifiques perçoivent ces gouttelettes et ouvre de nouvelles voies pour concevoir des médicaments. Au lieu de traiter les condensats comme des amas sans forme, les médicaments pourraient cibler leurs échafaudages internes pour lutter contre des maladies comme le cancer et la SLA.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier comment cette structure filamentaire fonctionne dans les cellules humaines et comment développer des médicaments pour réparer les problèmes des condensats. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux traitements qui empêchent l'accumulation de protéines nuisibles ou aident à mieux contrôler la croissance cellulaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte d’un échafaudage protéique caché dans les gouttelettes cellulaires révèle de nouvelles cibles pour traiter le cancer et les maladies neurodégénératives comme la SLA.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren estructura oculta dentro de gotas celulares relacionada con cáncer y ELA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Dentro de nuestras células, pequeños grupos en forma de gotas llamados condensados biomoleculares ayudan a manejar tareas importantes como hacer proteínas y eliminar desechos. Antes, los científicos pensaban que estas gotas eran simples masas sin forma especial.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Scripps Research encontraron que algunas de estas gotas en realidad tienen un armazón interno hecho de finos hilos de proteínas. Estudiaron una proteína bacteriana llamada PopZ usando un método de imagen especial, parecido a una tomografía computarizada en miniatura. Vieron que las proteínas PopZ forman filamentos que crean una estructura dentro de las gotas. Cuando se eliminaron los filamentos, las células no pudieron crecer ni dividirse bien.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que los condensados tienen una estructura clara necesaria para su función. Cambia la forma en que los científicos ven estas gotas y abre nuevas formas de diseñar medicamentos. En vez de tratar los condensados como masas sin forma, los medicamentos podrían dirigirse a sus armazones internos para ayudar a combatir enfermedades como el cáncer y la ELA.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos investigarán cómo funciona esta estructura filamentosa en células humanas y cómo desarrollar medicamentos para corregir problemas en los condensados. Esto podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos que eviten la acumulación de proteínas dañinas o ayuden a controlar mejor el crecimiento celular.

**Resumen en una frase** – Encontrar un armazón proteico oculto dentro de gotas celulares revela nuevas dianas para tratar el cáncer y enfermedades neurodegenerativas como la ELA.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate network of glowing, thread-like filaments weaves a translucent, droplet-shaped scaffold floating gently in a soft, warm light, surrounded by subtle abstract shapes symbolizing cellular activity and balance, rendered in delicate natural tones with fine painterly detail.

articles.title: Ultra-sensitive CAR T cells eliminate hard-to-treat tumours in mice
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00622-9A proof-of-concept study opens up an avenue for treating solid tumours that express low levels of a target antigen.
articles.content:
Engineered immune cells can identify ovarian cancer cells (pictured) that might otherwise escape detection.Credit: Steve Gschmeissner/Science Photo Library

Immune cells engineered to detect extremely low levels of a target antigen have eliminated kidney, ovarian and pancreatic tumours in mice1 — a feat that has been near impossible using conventional CAR-T immunotherapies.

Unlike blood cancers, solid tumours are hard to treat using CAR T cells because the tumours are dense, difficult to access and lack a common antigen target on every cell. At least, that’s been the story until now.

A paradigm-shifting study published in Science today, overturns this theory by demonstrating that at least one antigen, CD70, is expressed on 100% of cancer cells in some solid tumours. However, it is often expressed at such low levels that the antigens cannot be detected through normal methods. The authors of the study are now planning to seek funding for a phase I safety trial in humans.

“It’s a jump,” says Sophie Hanina, an immunologist at Columbia University Irving Medical Center in New York City who spearheaded the research. “It’s targeting the unseen.” Those kinds of jumps can only happen when they are guided by intuition, Hanina says.

It was a hunch that led Hanina to double-check whether seemingly CD70-free cancer cells expressed residual amounts of the protein. She found that kidney tumours that were genetically engineered to lack CD70 seemed dark when using an optical imaging technique known as confocal microscopy. But tumours that were negative for CD70 on every other conventional test displayed a faint signal for the protein when using this imaging technique. This “hinted that there might be low-level expression retained”, she says.

Hanina found that the levels of CD70 antigens were reduced – but not completely absent – in some cancer cells because they were being silenced.

Antigen escape

One of the many reasons that CAR-T-cell therapies fail in solid tumours is because of ‘antigen escape’ — in which the engineered immune cells attack all cancer cells that have high expression of a target antigen, but miss those with low or zero expression of that antigen, says Michel Sadelain, a co-author of the study and a cell engineer at Columbia University Irving Medical Center, who helped launch CAR-T-cell therapy in the 1990s.

To tackle this problem, Sadelain’s laboratory invented an ultra-sensitive CAR T cell2 that can target cancer cells that have 10–50 times lower concentrations of antigens than those that can be treated with existing cellular therapies.

Now the subject of a patent application, this innovative CAR T cell – called a CD70-targeted HLA-independent T cell (HIT) receptor – is a fusion between a synthetic CAR (or chimeric antigen receptor) and a natural T cell receptor. The CD70-targeted HIT receptor has the same receptiveness for CD70 but it also has enhanced internal signalling to make the T cell more responsive when it detects tiny amounts of this antigen.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in cancer immunotherapy, demonstrating that engineered ultra-sensitive CAR T cells can eliminate hard-to-treat solid tumors in mice by detecting very low levels of a target antigen. This advancement addresses a major challenge in treating solid tumors, has broad implications for cancer treatment, and is supported by detailed scientific context and plans for human trials.
articles.personality_title: New ultra-sensitive CAR T cells destroy tough solid tumors in mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Solid tumors like kidney, ovarian, and pancreatic cancers are hard to treat with current CAR T cell therapies. This is because these tumors have very few or hidden targets for the immune cells to recognize.

**What happened** – Researchers at Columbia University created a new type of engineered immune cell, called CD70-targeted HIT receptor T cells, that can detect tiny amounts of a protein called CD70 on cancer cells. These ultra-sensitive CAR T cells were able to eliminate tumors in mice that were previously hard to treat.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that solid tumors do have a common target, CD70, but it is often present at very low levels that older treatments could not detect. By overcoming this, the new CAR T cells can attack cancer cells that would normally escape treatment. This could change how solid tumors are treated in the future.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to seek funding to start safety tests of these ultra-sensitive CAR T cells in humans. If successful, this could lead to new therapies for patients with difficult solid tumors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed a new type of CAR T cell that can find and kill solid tumor cells by detecting very low levels of a key protein, offering hope for better cancer treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles cellules CAR T ultra-sensibles détruisent les tumeurs solides difficiles chez la souris
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tumeurs solides comme celles des reins, des ovaires et du pancréas sont difficiles à traiter avec les thérapies CAR T actuelles, car ces tumeurs ont peu ou pas de cibles visibles pour les cellules immunitaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Columbia ont créé un nouveau type de cellule immunitaire modifiée, appelée récepteur HIT ciblant CD70, capable de détecter de très faibles quantités d’une protéine appelée CD70 sur les cellules cancéreuses. Ces cellules CAR T ultra-sensibles ont réussi à éliminer des tumeurs chez la souris qui étaient auparavant difficiles à traiter.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les tumeurs solides possèdent une cible commune, CD70, souvent présente en très faible quantité que les traitements anciens ne pouvaient pas détecter. En surmontant cette difficulté, les nouvelles cellules CAR T peuvent attaquer des cellules cancéreuses qui échappaient normalement au traitement, ce qui pourrait changer la façon de traiter ces tumeurs.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit de demander des financements pour commencer des essais de sécurité chez l’humain. Si ces essais réussissent, cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements pour les patients atteints de tumeurs solides difficiles.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont développé un nouveau type de cellule CAR T capable de détecter et tuer les cellules de tumeurs solides en repérant de très faibles niveaux d’une protéine clé, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs traitements contre le cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas células CAR T ultra-sensibles eliminan tumores sólidos difíciles en ratones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los tumores sólidos como los de riñón, ovario y páncreas son difíciles de tratar con las terapias CAR T actuales porque tienen pocas o ninguna diana visible para las células inmunitarias.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Columbia crearon un nuevo tipo de célula inmunitaria modificada, llamada receptor HIT dirigido a CD70, que puede detectar cantidades muy pequeñas de una proteína llamada CD70 en las células cancerosas. Estas células CAR T ultra-sensibles lograron eliminar tumores en ratones que antes eran difíciles de tratar.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que los tumores sólidos tienen una diana común, CD70, que a menudo está presente en niveles muy bajos que los tratamientos antiguos no podían detectar. Al superar esto, las nuevas células CAR T pueden atacar células cancerosas que normalmente escapaban del tratamiento, lo que podría cambiar la forma de tratar estos tumores.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea buscar fondos para iniciar pruebas de seguridad en humanos. Si tienen éxito, esto podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para pacientes con tumores sólidos difíciles.

**Frase clave** – Científicos han desarrollado un nuevo tipo de célula CAR T que puede encontrar y destruir células de tumores sólidos al detectar niveles muy bajos de una proteína clave, ofreciendo esperanza para mejores tratamientos contra el cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a group of glowing, stylized immune cells with delicate antenna-like receptors gently illuminating and surrounding faintly glowing clusters of abstract ovarian cancer cells, depicted as softly textured, low-light spheres, set against a simple, natural background that emphasizes the sensitive detection and elimination of hidden tumor cells.

articles.title: World-first stem-cell therapy shows promise for treating spina bifida in the womb
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00602-zSix pregnant women participated in a small trial in which placenta-derived stem cells were applied to fetal spinal cords and the treatment was found to be safe.
articles.content:
An in utero treatment using placenta-derived stem cells could treat infants with a neural-tube condition called spina bifida. Credit: Simon Fraser/Science Photo Library

Stem cells applied to the exposed spinal cords of fetuses in utero could treat infants with a severe birth complication that affects movement and continence. Researchers report that the therapy is safe, following a small clinical trial in six people1.

Spina bifida, a condition in which the spinal cord is not properly enclosed during gestation, affects fewer than 1 in 1,000 births globally, but occurs at higher rates in low-income countries. The most severe form, called myelomeningocele, can cause excess fluid accumulation in the brain and potential brain damage, as well as paralysis and issues with bladder and bowel control.

Fetal and neonatal surgeon Diana Farmer at the University of California, Davis, led the Cellular Therapy for In Utero Repair of Myelomeningocele (CuRE) trial to assess whether a treatment using stem cells derived from the placenta is feasible and whether it causes harm to pregnant people or their newborns.

Six pregnant women underwent surgery at 24–25 weeks of gestation. During the procedure, stem cells generated from donated placentas were applied to the fetuses’ exposed spinal cords directly. The authors say there were no complications during the surgeries, and the newborns were delivered at around 34 weeks of gestation with no signs of infection, cerebrospinal fluid leakage or tumour growth. Farmer says there were concerns that the stem cells could become cancerous.

All of the newborns showed signs that a complication of their condition called hindbrain herniation — in which the back of the brain descends into the neck and blocks the circulation of cerebrospinal fluid — had been reversed.

“It’s exciting and it’s important that we’re advancing in the field and that we’re looking at novel therapies,” says Clare Whitehead, a fetal-medicine specialist at the Royal Women’s Hospital in Melbourne, Australia. It is also reassuring that the therapy did not cause harm, but it is still too early to know whether it will be beneficial. “With fetal therapy, we have to be very cautious.” Positive results in studies with a small number of participants don’t always translate to benefits in larger populations, she adds.

A long time coming

Currently, women whose fetuses are diagnosed with myelomeningocele can undergo surgery during pregnancy to close the fetus’ spinal cord defect. But Farmer — who led the trial showing that this in utero surgery was beneficial2 — says that nearly 60% of children are unable to walk or move independently following the procedure. This happens because the surgery does not reverse damage to neurons in the spinal cord caused by exposure to amniotic fluid while the fetus is in utero.

Diana Farmer (right), a fetal and neonatal surgeon at the University of California, Davis, led the Cellular Therapy for In Utero Repair of Myelomeningocele (CuRE) trial. Credit: UC Davis

Farmer’s team began testing whether stem cells could reverse spinal cord damage shortly after stem-cell researcher Shinya Yamanaka received a Nobel prize in 2012 for his work using induced pluripotent stem cells, which are mature cells that have been reprogramed to be able to develop into almost any type of cell in the body. Although using induced pluripotent stem cells did not work, Farmer’s team eventually succeeded using stem cells derived from the placenta, first in cell-based studies and then in sheep models. Sheep that underwent surgery to close the defect and received the stem cells were able to stand independently and walk, whereas the control sheep that received only the surgery were not3.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a pioneering clinical trial demonstrating the safety of a novel in utero stem-cell therapy for spina bifida, a severe birth defect. This therapy has the potential to improve outcomes for affected infants by reversing neurological damage before birth, representing a significant medical breakthrough with broad implications for fetal medicine and disability prevention.
articles.personality_title: New stem-cell treatment shows safety in unborn babies with spina bifida
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Spina bifida is a birth defect where a baby’s spinal cord doesn’t form properly during pregnancy. This can cause problems with movement and bladder control after birth. Currently, surgery before birth helps but doesn’t fully fix the damage.

**What happened** – Six pregnant women took part in a trial where doctors used stem cells from the placenta and applied them directly to the babies’ exposed spinal cords during pregnancy. The treatment happened around 24 to 25 weeks of pregnancy, and the babies were born safely at about 34 weeks. No harmful side effects like infections or tumors were seen.

**Impact** – This is the first time this kind of stem-cell therapy has been tested in humans before birth for spina bifida. The treatment seemed to reverse a serious brain problem linked to the condition. It shows that the therapy is safe and could protect babies’ nerves better than current surgery alone.

**What's next step** – More studies with larger groups of patients are needed to see if the treatment really helps babies walk and live better lives. Researchers will continue to test how well the stem cells repair nerve damage and improve outcomes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new stem-cell treatment applied before birth has been shown safe and may help heal nerve damage in babies with spina bifida.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau traitement par cellules souches sûr pour les bébés atteints de spina bifida avant la naissance
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le spina bifida est un défaut de naissance où la moelle épinière du bébé ne se forme pas correctement pendant la grossesse. Cela peut causer des problèmes de mouvement et de contrôle de la vessie après la naissance. La chirurgie avant la naissance aide, mais ne répare pas complètement les dommages.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Six femmes enceintes ont participé à un essai où des médecins ont appliqué des cellules souches issues du placenta directement sur la moelle épinière exposée des bébés pendant la grossesse. Le traitement a eu lieu vers la 24e-25e semaine de grossesse, et les bébés sont nés en toute sécurité vers la 34e semaine. Aucun effet secondaire dangereux comme une infection ou une tumeur n’a été observé.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un tel traitement par cellules souches est testé avant la naissance chez l’humain pour le spina bifida. Le traitement a semblé inverser un grave problème cérébral lié à la maladie. Cela montre que la thérapie est sûre et pourrait mieux protéger les nerfs des bébés que la chirurgie actuelle.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres études avec plus de patients sont nécessaires pour vérifier si le traitement aide vraiment les bébés à marcher et à mieux vivre. Les chercheurs continueront à tester comment les cellules souches réparent les nerfs et améliorent les résultats.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau traitement par cellules souches appliqué avant la naissance a montré sa sécurité et pourrait aider à réparer les lésions nerveuses chez les bébés atteints de spina bifida.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo tratamiento con células madre seguro para bebés con espina bífida antes de nacer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La espina bífida es un defecto de nacimiento donde la médula espinal del bebé no se forma bien durante el embarazo. Esto puede causar problemas para moverse y controlar la vejiga después del nacimiento. La cirugía antes de nacer ayuda, pero no repara todo el daño.

**Qué pasó** – Seis mujeres embarazadas participaron en un ensayo donde los médicos aplicaron células madre del placenta directamente sobre la médula espinal expuesta de los bebés durante el embarazo. El tratamiento se hizo entre las semanas 24 y 25, y los bebés nacieron sanos alrededor de la semana 34. No hubo efectos secundarios graves como infecciones o tumores.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se prueba este tipo de terapia con células madre en humanos antes de nacer para la espina bífida. El tratamiento pareció revertir un problema grave en el cerebro relacionado con la condición. Esto muestra que la terapia es segura y podría proteger mejor los nervios de los bebés que la cirugía actual.

**Próximo paso** – Se necesitan estudios con más pacientes para saber si el tratamiento realmente ayuda a los bebés a caminar y vivir mejor. Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo las células madre reparan el daño nervioso y mejoran los resultados.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo tratamiento con células madre aplicado antes del nacimiento ha demostrado ser seguro y podría ayudar a reparar el daño nervioso en bebés con espina bífida.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a delicate unborn lamb nestled in a soft womb-like environment, with a glowing, translucent placenta gently enveloping and nurturing the lamb’s exposed, fragile spinal cord area, symbolizing healing and protection through stem-cell therapy, rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft pastels.

articles.title: Shingles vaccine may slow biological aging and reduce inflammation
articles.summary: A shingles shot might do more than prevent a painful rash — it could actually help slow down the aging process. In a large national study of more than 3,800 Americans age 70 and older, those who received the shingles vaccine showed slower biological aging compared to those who didn’t. Researchers found lower levels of chronic inflammation and slower changes in gene activity linked to aging, suggesting the vaccine may calm the body’s “inflammaging” — the low-grade inflammation tied to heart disease, frailty, and cognitive decline.
articles.content:
A shingles vaccine may do more than prevent a painful rash. New research from the USC Leonard Davis School of Gerontology suggests it could also be associated with slower biological aging in older adults.

Scientists analyzed information from the nationally representative U.S. Health and Retirement Study, focusing on more than 3,800 adults who were age 70 and older in 2016. After accounting for differences in health status and demographic factors, those who had received the shingles vaccine showed slower overall biological aging compared to participants who were not vaccinated.

What Is Shingles and Who Is at Risk

Shingles, also known as herpes zoster, is a painful rash with blisters that develops when the virus that causes chickenpox, varicella zoster, becomes active again later in life. Anyone who has had chickenpox carries the virus and can develop shingles. Although it can affect younger adults, the risk rises significantly after age 50 and is higher in immunocompromized individuals. Vaccination, which has typically been recommended for older adults, reduces the likelihood of shingles and lowers the risk of postherpetic neuralgia, or long-term nerve pain that can persist after the rash clears.

Vaccines are designed to prevent infections, but growing evidence suggests they may offer broader health effects. Research Associate Professor of Gerontology Jung Ki Kim, the study's first author, noted that earlier studies have linked adult vaccines, including shingles and influenza shots, to lower risks of dementia and other neurodegenerative diseases.

"This study adds to emerging evidence that vaccines could play a role in promoting healthy aging by modulating biological systems beyond infection prevention," she said.

Biological Aging Versus Chronological Age

Biological aging refers to how well the body's systems are functioning, rather than the number of years a person has lived. Two people who are both 65 years old can have very different biological profiles. One may have organs and systems that resemble those of a younger person, while the other may show signs of accelerated aging.

To better understand these differences, Kim and coauthor Eileen Crimmins, USC University Professor and AARP Professor of Gerontology, evaluated seven markers of biological aging:

inflammation

innate immunity (the body's general defenses against infection)

adaptive immunity (responses to specific pathogens after exposure or vaccination)

cardiovascular hemodynamics (blood flow)

neurodegeneration

epigenetic aging (changes in how genes are turned "off" or "on")

transcriptomic aging (changes in how genes are transcribed into RNA used to create proteins)

They also combined these measurements into an overall biological aging score.

Lower Inflammation and Slower Cellular Aging

On average, vaccinated participants had lower levels of inflammation, slower epigenetic and transcriptomic aging, and better overall biological aging scores than those who were not vaccinated. These findings help clarify how immune health may influence the aging process.

Long-lasting, low-grade inflammation is known to contribute to many conditions associated with aging, including heart disease, frailty, and cognitive decline. Researchers often refer to this persistent inflammation as "inflammaging," Kim explained.

"By helping to reduce this background inflammation -- possibly by preventing reactivation of the virus that causes shingles, the vaccine may play a role in supporting healthier aging," she said. "While the exact biological mechanisms remain to be understood, the potential for vaccination to reduce inflammation makes it a promising addition to broader strategies aimed at promoting resilience and slowing age-related decline."

Benefits That May Last for Years

The researchers also examined how long ago participants had received their vaccine. Those who were vaccinated four or more years before providing a blood sample still showed slower epigenetic, transcriptomic and overall biological aging compared to unvaccinated individuals. This suggests the potential effects may persist for several years.

"These findings indicate that shingles vaccination influences key domains linked to the aging process," Crimmins said. "While further research is needed to replicate and extend these findings, especially using longitudinal and experimental designs, our study adds to a growing body of work suggesting that vaccines may play a role in healthy aging strategies beyond solely preventing acute illness."

About the Study

The study, "Association between shingles vaccination and slower biological aging: Evidence from a U.S. population-based cohort study," was published in the Journals of Gerontology, Series A: Biological Sciences and Medical Sciences on January 20, 2026. This work was supported by the National Institute on Aging at the National Institutes of Health (P30 AG017265); the Health and Retirement Study is supported by National Institute on Aging (U01AG009740).
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific finding that the shingles vaccine may slow biological aging and reduce chronic inflammation, which has broad implications for healthy aging in older adults. The study is large-scale, nationally representative, and provides detailed context on how vaccination could contribute to reducing age-related diseases, offering a hopeful advancement in health and longevity.
articles.personality_title: Shingles vaccine linked to slower biological aging and less inflammation in older adults
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Shingles is a painful rash caused by a virus that stays in the body after chickenpox. It mostly affects people over 50 and can cause long-lasting nerve pain. Vaccines help prevent shingles, especially in older adults, but new research suggests they might also slow down the body’s aging process.

**What happened** – Researchers studied more than 3,800 Americans aged 70 and older using data from a national health survey. They found that those who had received the shingles vaccine showed slower biological aging compared to those who did not get the vaccine. The study measured things like inflammation and changes in gene activity, which are signs of how the body ages at a cellular level.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows the shingles vaccine might do more than just stop a rash. It could reduce “inflammaging,” a type of ongoing inflammation linked to heart problems, weakness, and memory loss. People vaccinated even four years earlier still showed signs of slower aging, suggesting the vaccine’s benefits last a long time.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to do more research to understand exactly how the vaccine slows aging and to see if other vaccines have similar effects. This could lead to new ways of helping older adults stay healthier for longer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Getting the shingles vaccine may help older adults age more slowly by reducing inflammation and protecting their cells over time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le vaccin contre le zona pourrait ralentir le vieillissement biologique et réduire l'inflammation chez les personnes âgées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le zona est une éruption douloureuse causée par un virus qui reste dans le corps après la varicelle. Il touche surtout les personnes de plus de 50 ans et peut provoquer des douleurs nerveuses durables. Les vaccins aident à prévenir le zona, surtout chez les personnes âgées, mais de nouvelles recherches suggèrent qu’ils pourraient aussi ralentir le vieillissement du corps.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié plus de 3 800 Américains âgés de 70 ans et plus à partir d’une enquête nationale sur la santé. Ils ont découvert que ceux qui avaient reçu le vaccin contre le zona vieillissaient biologiquement plus lentement que ceux qui ne l’avaient pas reçu. L’étude a mesuré des signes comme l’inflammation et les changements dans l’activité des gènes, qui montrent comment le corps vieillit au niveau cellulaire.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre que le vaccin contre le zona fait peut-être plus que prévenir une éruption. Il pourrait réduire « l’inflammaging », une inflammation persistante liée aux problèmes cardiaques, à la faiblesse et à la perte de mémoire. Même ceux vaccinés il y a quatre ans montraient des signes de vieillissement plus lent, ce qui suggère que les effets durent longtemps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques souhaitent approfondir la recherche pour comprendre comment le vaccin ralentit le vieillissement et voir si d’autres vaccins ont des effets similaires. Cela pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles façons d’aider les personnes âgées à rester en bonne santé plus longtemps.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Se faire vacciner contre le zona pourrait aider les personnes âgées à vieillir plus lentement en réduisant l’inflammation et en protégeant leurs cellules dans le temps.
articles.personality_title_es: La vacuna contra el herpes zóster podría ralentizar el envejecimiento biológico y reducir la inflamación en adultos mayores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El herpes zóster es una erupción dolorosa causada por un virus que permanece en el cuerpo después de la varicela. Afecta principalmente a personas mayores de 50 años y puede causar dolor nervioso prolongado. Las vacunas ayudan a prevenir el herpes zóster, especialmente en adultos mayores, pero nuevas investigaciones sugieren que también podrían ralentizar el proceso de envejecimiento del cuerpo.

Qué pasó – Investigadores estudiaron a más de 3,800 estadounidenses de 70 años o más usando datos de una encuesta nacional de salud. Encontraron que quienes recibieron la vacuna contra el herpes zóster mostraron un envejecimiento biológico más lento que quienes no se vacunaron. El estudio midió niveles de inflamación y cambios en la actividad genética, señales de cómo envejece el cuerpo a nivel celular.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la vacuna podría hacer más que prevenir una erupción. Podría reducir la “inflamación crónica” relacionada con problemas del corazón, debilidad y pérdida de memoria. Incluso las personas vacunadas hace cuatro años seguían mostrando signos de envejecimiento más lento, lo que sugiere que los beneficios duran mucho tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren investigar más para entender cómo la vacuna ralentiza el envejecimiento y ver si otras vacunas tienen efectos parecidos. Esto podría abrir nuevas formas de ayudar a los adultos mayores a mantenerse saludables por más tiempo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Recibir la vacuna contra el herpes zóster podría ayudar a los adultos mayores a envejecer más lentamente al reducir la inflamación y proteger sus células con el tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an ancient, sturdy tree with vibrant, healthy leaves symbolizing slowed biological aging, surrounded by softly glowing protective shields shaped like stylized vaccine capsules gently enveloping the tree’s trunk, with subtle golden threads weaving through the branches representing reduced inflammation and strengthened immune resilience, all rendered in natural, earthy tones.
